data.title: Quantum Computing Insider Sold Shares Worth $4,590,282, According to a Recent SEC Filing
data.description:
Christopher Boehmler, Chief Financial Officer, on June 09, 2025, sold 272,445 shares in Quantum Computing for $4,590,282. Following the Form 4 filing with the SEC, Boehmler has control over a total of 47,565 common shares of the company, with 47,565 shares held directly.

SEC Filing:

www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/1758009/000121390025053571/xslF345X05/ownership.xml

data.title: UK 10-Year Gilt Yield Falls to 5-Week Low
data.description:
The UK 10-year gilt yield fell to 4.538%, its lowest level in five weeks, after data showed the UK economy shrank more than expected in April.

GDP contracted by 0.3%, raising fears of a slowdown and prompting traders to increase bets on Bank of England rate cuts.

UK exports to the US also posted a record drop, following recent tariff actions.

Despite the weak data, the BoE is still expected to keep interest rates steady at next week’s meeting.

Meanwhile, global markets were rattled by renewed trade tensions.

President Donald Trump said he plans to send letters outlining unilateral tariffs within two weeks, ahead of a July 9 deadline. “At a certain point, we’re just going to send letters out,” Trump stated, indicating that countries will have to accept or reject the terms without further negotiation.

data.title: i-mobile Nine-Month Profit Rises 11.6%
data.description:
i-mobile Co.,Ltd reported a 11.6% year-on-year increase in profit attributable to owners to 2.91 billion yen for the nine months ended April 30, 2025, according to a Wednesday filing on the Tokyo Stock Exchange.

Earnings per share came in at 50.25 yen, up from 45.35 yen per share a year ago. This reflects a three-for-one stock split in November 2023.

Net sales rose 14.2% to 18.65 billion yen.

The Tokyo-based digital advertising and media company maintained its full-year forecast, projecting attributable profit to rise 21.9% to 2.95 billion yen, with EPS seen at 51.20 yen and revenue expected to grow 22.8% to 23 billion yen.

i-mobile plans to raise its year-end dividend to 26 yen per share, up from 22 yen the previous year.

data.title: Pod Point Group Signs GBP11 Million Takeover Deal with EDF Energy Customers
data.description:
Pod Point Group said Thursday it agreed to an all-cash takeover by EDF Energy Customers for 10.6 million pounds sterling, or 0.065 pound per share.

EDF, which currently holds a 53% stake in Pod Point, will take full ownership of the electric vehicle charging services group by implementing a court-sanctioned scheme of arrangement.

The scheme is expected to take effect in the third quarter, subject to customary closing conditions.

data.title: Deutsche Bank's deal-making business weaker than expected, CEO says
data.description:
Deutsche Bank's origination and advisory business is not as strong as the German bank had expected at the beginning of the year, CEO Christian Sewing said on Thursday.

Sewing said at a financial conference that the deal-making business will be weaker in the second quarter than executives had planned at the start of 2025 as companies postpone decisions in the wake of U.S. tariff policies.

"Will it be weaker than we initially thought? Yes. But to be honest, we talk about a lot of delayed, but not cancelled, deals," Sewing said.

Deal-making has slowed this year as U.S. President Donald Trump's tariffs on trading partners fanned turmoil in markets and sparked concerns about slowing economic growth. Other big banks, including Bank of America , have also warned of a slowdown in the business.

Sewing said he was optimistic about Deutsche's other business lines, like the corporate bank and retail bank, and Deutsche Bank is sticking to its targets, he said.

This is a crucial year for Deutshe Bank, Germany's largest lender, as it faces a deadline to meet ambitious targets on costs and profitability.

data.title: Turkish industrial output falls 3.1% month-on-month in April
data.description:
Turkey's industrial production fell 3.1% month-on-month in Arpil on a seasonally and calendar-adjusted basis, official data showed on Thursday, with the manufacturing index falling 3.4%.

Industrial output climbed 3.3% year-on-year in Arpil, according to the Turkish Statistical Institute.

data.title: RBA Warns Against Bond Market Complacency in Risk-Filled World
data.description:
By James Glynn

SYDNEY--The Reserve Bank of Australia has lauded the depth of the country's bond market and its ability to withstand recent shocks, but warned about complacency should the upheavals that roiled markets in April make a return.

The sharp rise in government bond yields that followed the Trump administration's announcement in early April of tariffs targeted at its major trading partners, was mirrored in Australia, but ultimately the local market was able to adjust and recover, David Jacobs, the head of domestic markets at the RBA told a debt conference in Tokyo.

"We saw a repricing, but not a broad-based shift to cash. Sellers were able to find willing buyers, and Australian governments continued issuing, though at a slower pace," he said.

Derivatives markets were also resilient, including bond futures, which was in contrast with the early days of the pandemic, when markets became dysfunctional and threatened broader financial stability, Jacobs added.

Still a key reason that markets stabilized quickly in April was the pause on the implementation of tariffs, which "suggests little room for complacency," he added.

Australia's level of vulnerability to the global trade war is complicated by its heavy reliance on trade with China, which buys around one-third of its exports. If China's economy were to slow as global supply chains are cut by trade uncertainty, the impact would be felt quickly in Canberra.

"The events in early April were somewhat dramatic, though brief, and illustrated how changes in the global economic system will play out quickest in capital markets," Jacobs added.

The U.S. administration's announcement of larger and broader tariffs than expected, and the response of other governments, saw markets rapidly reassess the outlook.

"Some large positions in international government bond markets, often associated with leverage, were unwound relatively quickly leading to a sharp rise in yields and thinner liquidity," he said.

Write to James Glynn at james.glynn@wsj.com

data.title: FTSE 100 Little Changed After Poor GDP Data
data.description:
The FTSE 100 traded flat to slightly lower on Thursday after UK GDP data showed a sharper-than-expected contraction of 0.3% in April, raising concerns over economic momentum.

Adding to market unease, US President Donald Trump reignited trade worries, stating he plans to send letters to countries in the next two weeks outlining unilateral tariffs, ahead of a July 9 deadline.

Trump warned that affected nations would face a “take it or leave it” offer.

UK exports to the US saw a record drop in April following earlier tariff actions.

However, the weak GDP print fueled speculation that the Bank of England could cut interest rates sooner than expected.

On the corporate front, Tesco maintained its profit guidance and reported a 4.7% rise in like-for-like sales for the 13 weeks ending May 24.

Growth was driven by strong performance in its UK and Ireland businesses, especially in fresh food and non-food categories like home and clothing, aided by warmer weather.

data.title: Major Gulf markets retreat on geopolitics
data.description:
Major stock markets in the Gulf fell in early trade on Thursday amid uncertainty following the U.S. decision to relocate personnel from the Middle East ahead of nuclear talks with Iran.

U.S. President Donald Trump said on Wednesday U.S. personnel were being moved out of the Middle East because "it could be a dangerous place," adding that the United States would not allow Iran to have a nuclear weapon.

Reuters reported on Wednesday that the U.S. is preparing a partial evacuation of its Iraqi embassy and will allow military dependents to leave locations around the Middle East due to heightened security risks in the region, according to U.S. and Iraqi sources.

Saudi Arabia's benchmark index dropped 1.3% as almost all its constituents were in negative territory including Al Rajhi Bank , which was down 0.6%.

Among other losers, oil giant Saudi Aramco was down 0.4%.

The decision by the U.S. to evacuate some personnel comes at a volatile moment in the region. Trump's efforts to reach a nuclear deal with Iran appear to be deadlocked and U.S. intelligence indicates that Israel has been making preparations for a strike against Iran's nuclear facilities.

Iranian Defence Minister Aziz Nasirzadeh said on Wednesday that if Iran was subjected to strikes it would retaliate by hitting U.S. bases in the region.

Dubai's main share index (.DDFMGI) retreated 1.7%, its biggest intraday fall since April, dragged down by losses across sectors and led by a 3% slide in blue-chip developer Emaar Properties .

In Abu Dhabi, the index fell 1%, hit by a 2% fall in ADNOC Gas .

The Qatari index traded 0.8% lower, with petrochemical maker Industries Qatar losing 1%.
